title: Agents Will Multiply Tool Use, Not Kill It: Huang's Contrarian Software Thesis

source.original: Jensen Huang – Will Nvidia's moat persist?

body_html:
While much of the market fears that AI will commoditize enterprise software companies, Jensen Huang argues the opposite trajectory is unfolding. His thesis centers on a distinction between tool makers and workflow codification systems—and he believes the tool makers are about to see explosive growth in usage, not decline.

The logic is straightforward: today, the number of instances of tools like Synopsys Design Compiler or Cadence layout tools is limited by the number of human engineers who can operate them. As AI agents become capable tool users, Huang expects the number of concurrent instances of these tools to skyrocket. Agents will explore design spaces, run simulations, and invoke software at a pace no human workforce could match. Each agent becomes a new 'seat' for existing software.

Huang acknowledges this hasn't happened yet for a simple reason: agents aren't good enough at using tools yet. But he sees convergence from both directions—software companies will build agents tailored to their tools, and general-purpose agents will improve until they can operate existing interfaces. The combination of both forces will drive adoption.

This framing has significant implications for how investors and builders should think about AI's impact on the software ecosystem. Rather than a zero-sum displacement where AI replaces software, Huang envisions a multiplicative relationship where AI dramatically increases the consumption of existing tools. It's a thesis that bets on complementarity over substitution.

The deeper strategic point is that this explosion in tool usage requires compute—lots of it. Every agent running a design rule checker or floor planner needs GPU cycles. Huang's software thesis isn't just a defense of enterprise SaaS; it's a demand story for Nvidia's hardware, creating yet another flywheel in the ecosystem he's constructing.
